source: CIS GOOGLE CLOUD PLATFORM FOUNDATION BENCHMARK V4.0.0
title: Ensure ‘Log_statement’ Database Flag for Cloud SQL PostgreSQL Instance Is Set Appropriately

input_row.source: CIS GOOGLE CLOUD PLATFORM FOUNDATION BENCHMARK V4.0.0
input_row.title: Ensure ‘Log_statement’ Database Flag for Cloud SQL PostgreSQL Instance Is Set Appropriately
input_row.description: The value of log_statement flag determined the SQL statements that are logged. Valid values are: • none • ddl • mod • all The value ddl logs all data definition statements. The value mod logs all ddl statements, plus data-modifying statements. The statements are logged after a basic parsing is done and statement type is determined, thus this does not logs statements with errors. When using extended query protocol, logging occurs after an Execute message is received and values of the Bind parameters are included. A value of 'ddl' is recommended unless otherwise directed by your organization's logging policy.
input_row.rationale: Auditing helps in forensic analysis. If log_statement is not set to the correct value, too many statements may be logged leading to issues in finding the relevant information from the logs, or too few statements may be logged with relevant information missing from the logs. Setting log_statement to align with your organization's security and logging policies facilitates later auditing and review of database activities. This recommendation is applicable to PostgreSQL database instances. Impact: Turning on logging will increase the required storage over time. Mismanaged logs may cause your storage costs to increase. Setting custom flags via command line on certain instances will cause all omitted flags to be reset to defaults. This may cause you to lose custom flags and could result in unforeseen complications or instance restarts. Because of this, it is recommended you apply these flags changes during a period of low usage.
input_row.audit: From Google Cloud Console 1. Go to the Cloud SQL Instances page in the Google Cloud Console by visiting https://console.cloud.google.com/sql/instances. 2. Select the instance to open its Instance Overview page 3. Go to Configuration card 4. Under Database flags, check the value of log_statement flag is set to appropriately. From Google Cloud CLI 1. Use the below command for every Cloud SQL PostgreSQL database instance to verify the value of log_statement gcloud sql instances list --format=json | jq '.[].settings.databaseFlags[] | select(.name=="log_statement")|.value'
input_row.remediation: From Google Cloud Console 1. Go to the Cloud SQL Instances page in the Google Cloud Console by visiting https://console.cloud.google.com/sql/instances. 2. Select the PostgreSQL instance for which you want to enable the database flag. 3. Click Edit. 4. Scroll down to the Flags section. 5. To set a flag that has not been set on the instance before, click Add a Database Flag, choose the flag log_statement from the drop-down menu and set appropriate value. 6. Click Save to save your changes. 7. Confirm your changes under Flags on the Overview page. From Google Cloud CLI 1. Configure the log_statement database flag for every Cloud SQL PosgreSQL database instance using the below command. gcloud sql instances patch <INSTANCE_NAME> --database-flags log_statement=<ddl|mod|all|none> Note : This command will overwrite all database flags previously set. To keep those and add new ones, include the values for all flags you want set on the instance; any flag not specifically included is set to its default value. For flags that do not take a value, specify the flag name followed by an equals sign ("="). Default Value: none References: 1. https://cloud.google.com/sql/docs/postgres/flags 2. https://www.postgresql.org/docs/current/runtime-config-logging.html#GUC-LOG- STATEMENT Additional Information: WARNING: This patch modifies database flag values, which may require your instance to be restarted. Check the list of supported flags - https://cloud.google.com/sql/docs/postgres/flags - to see if your instance will be restarted when this patch is submitted. Note : some database flag settings can affect instance availability or stability and remove the instance from the Cloud SQL SLA. For information about these flags, see Operational Guidelines. Note : Configuring the above flag does not require restarting the Cloud SQL instance.
input_row.impact: Turning on logging will increase the required storage over time. Mismanaged logs may cause your storage costs to increase. Setting custom flags via command line on certain instances will cause all omitted flags to be reset to defaults. This may cause you to lose custom flags and could result in unforeseen complications or instance restarts. Because of this, it is recommended you apply these flags changes during a period of low usage.
input_row.references: 1. https://cloud.google.com/sql/docs/postgres/flags 2. https://www.postgresql.org/docs/current/runtime-config-logging.html#GUC-LOG- STATEMENT Additional Information: WARNING: This patch modifies database flag values, which may require your instance to be restarted. Check the list of supported flags - https://cloud.google.com/sql/docs/postgres/flags - to see if your instance will be restarted when this patch is submitted. Note : some database flag settings can affect instance availability or stability and remove the instance from the Cloud SQL SLA. For information about these flags, see Operational Guidelines. Note : Configuring the above flag does not require restarting the Cloud SQL instance.

step1_initial_assessment:
**AUDIT APPROACH:** Automated

**APPROACH DECISION:**
The control for ensuring the `log_statement` database flag for Cloud SQL PostgreSQL instances is appropriately set can be audited automatically using GCP APIs and CLI commands. The audit description provides a clear procedure through Google Cloud CLI to check the value of the `log_statement` flag. There is no requirement for SSH or RDP access, and all necessary checks can be executed using available SDK methods or CLI commands. The audit involves querying the configuration properties of Cloud SQL instances, which are standard GCP service properties accessible via APIs and SDK.

**PROGRAM NAME:** gcp_sql_postgresql_log_statement_flag_set

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:**
- **GCP API/SDK:** Use the Google Cloud SDK `gcloud sql instances describe` command to list and inspect database flags set on Cloud SQL PostgreSQL instances.
- **GCP CLI command:** 
  ```bash
  gcloud sql instances list --format=json | jq '.[].settings.databaseFlags[] | select(.name=="log_statement") | .value'
  ```
- **Resources to enumerate:** Query all Cloud SQL PostgreSQL instances in the GCP project.
- **Configuration properties/settings to validate:** 
  - Check the value of the `log_statement` flag. Valid values are `none`, `ddl`, `mod`, and `all`. Ensure it's set to `ddl` unless otherwise directed by the organization's policy.
- **Pass/Fail criteria:**
  - **Pass:** The `log_statement` flag is set to `ddl`, or in accordance with the organization's specific logging policy.
  - **Fail:** The `log_statement` flag is not set, or is set to an inappropriate value that does not align with the organization's logging policy. 

The automation effectively uses existing CLI tools to verify compliance without requiring manual intervention.
step2_review:
---

### 1. APPROACH VALIDATION: **AGREE**

- **Manual vs Automated classification:**  
  The classification as "Automated" is correct. The `log_statement` flag for Cloud SQL PostgreSQL instances is a configuration property that is exposed via the Cloud SQL Admin API and can be queried using the Google Cloud SDK (`gcloud sql instances describe`) and filtered using tools like `jq`.
- **GCP API/SDK/CLI feasibility:**  
  The check can be fully automated using GCP APIs (Cloud SQL Admin API), Google Cloud SDK (`gcloud`), and CLI scripting. No manual console review or SSH access is required.
- **Other GCP tools:**  
  While GCP Config (Config Connector, Config Validator) could also be used for policy-as-code, the CLI/API approach is direct and sufficient for this control.

---

### 2. TECHNICAL ACCURACY: **8/10**

- **Automation details:**  
  - The use of `gcloud sql instances list --format=json | jq ...` is technically valid for enumerating all Cloud SQL instances and extracting the `log_statement` flag.
  - However, the command as written will **not filter for PostgreSQL** instances only. It will include MySQL and SQL Server instances as well, which do not have the `log_statement` flag. This could result in errors or misleading output.
  - The command assumes all instances have the `databaseFlags` property set, which may not be true if no flags are configured.
- **Program name:**  
  - The name `gcp_sql_postgresql_log_statement_flag_set` is descriptive and follows the convention, but could be more explicit (see Improvements).
- **Pass/Fail criteria:**  
  - The criteria are generally correct, but should clarify that the organization's policy may require a value stricter than `ddl` (e.g., `mod` or `all`), and that the absence of the flag means default PostgreSQL behavior (which is `none`).
- **Completeness:**  
  - The assessment does not mention handling of instances with no `log_statement` flag set (default value), or how to handle multiple flags per instance.

---

### 3. IMPROVEMENTS

- **Filter for PostgreSQL instances:**  
  - Use `gcloud sql instances list --filter='databaseVersion~postgres'` to ensure only PostgreSQL instances are checked.
- **Handle missing flags:**  
  - The script should handle cases where `databaseFlags` is empty or missing, which means default behavior applies.
- **Alternative API method:**  
  - The Cloud SQL Admin API (`instances.list` and `instances.get`) can be used programmatically for large-scale or cross-project audits.
- **Program name suggestion:**  
  - To be more explicit: `gcp_sql_postgresql_log_statement_flag_value`
- **Output clarity:**  
  - The script should output the instance name along with the flag value for traceability.
- **Policy flexibility:**  
  - The check should allow for organization-specific policy (not just `ddl`), and this should be parameterized.
- **Sample improved command:**
    ```bash
    gcloud sql instances list --filter='databaseVersion~postgres' --format='value(name)' | while read -r INSTANCE; do
      VALUE=$(gcloud sql instances describe "$INSTANCE" --format="json" | jq -r '.settings.databaseFlags[]? | select(.name=="log_statement") | .value')
      echo "$INSTANCE: $VALUE"
    done
    ```

---

### 4. CONFIDENCE: **HIGH**

- The control is well-understood, and the automation approach is fundamentally sound. Minor technical improvements are needed for robustness and accuracy.

---

### 5. FINAL RECOMMENDATION: **Modify**

- **Keep the Automated classification.**
- **Modify the automation details** to:
  - Filter for PostgreSQL instances only.
  - Handle missing `databaseFlags` gracefully.
  - Output instance names for traceability.
  - Clarify pass/fail logic for unset flags and organization-specific policy.
  - Consider updating the program name to `gcp_sql_postgresql_log_statement_flag_value` for clarity.

---

**Summary Table:**

| Section             | Assessment                |
|---------------------|--------------------------|
| Approach Validation | AGREE                     |
| Technical Accuracy  | 8/10                      |
| Improvements        | See above                 |
| Confidence          | HIGH                      |
| Final Recommendation| Modify (see above)        |

---
